rationale_for_baseline_tier: Probable anterior shoulder dislocation with axillary nerve neuropraxia but INTACT distal circulation (warm pink hand, present wrist pulse, capillary refill <2 s) and stable haemodynamics. This requires the Emergency Department within hours for pre-reduction X-ray and closed reduction under procedural sedation — prolonged dislocation risks avascular necrosis and cartilage damage and prolongs nerve traction. Because the limb is well-perfused and the patient is stable and can be safely driven in by a companion, the correct tier is T4 (ED, hours, self-transport), NOT T5. It escalates to T5/000 only if the limb becomes neurovascularly compromised.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The hand turns pale, cold, blue or pulseless, or becomes completely/densely numb
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — if travelling, pull over and call 000 without delay
escalation_edges.rationale: This indicates the displaced bone has compromised the artery or nerve bundle to the arm — a limb-threatening emergency requiring an emergency ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your hand suddenly turns pale, cold or blue, or goes completely numb, pull over and call 000 straight away — it means the blood supply or nerves to your arm may be pinched off.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No safe private transport available, or pain too severe to move safely
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — arrange an ambulance for transport rather than delaying ED care
escalation_edges.rationale: The patient still needs the ED urgently; if they cannot be driven safely, ambulance transport is the appropriate route to the same destination.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your roommate can't drive you safely or the pain is too much to move, call 000 for an ambulance to take you to hospital.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening or spreading numbness/weakness in the arm or hand
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same visit — flag to ED staff on arrival; do not wait
escalation_edges.rationale: Progressing nerve symptoms indicate ongoing traction and reinforce the need for prompt reduction with documented neurovascular status.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the numbness or weakness in your arm or hand gets worse, tell the hospital team straight away when you arrive.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline pathway — obvious shoulder deformity with inability to move the arm
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the Emergency Department now (within hours) for X-ray and sedated reduction
escalation_edges.rationale: A suspected dislocation needs imaging to exclude fracture and reduction under sedation; every hour of delay increases joint damage. Do NOT attempt reduction at home.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Your shoulder looks out of joint — go straight to the Emergency Department now. Keep the arm still and supported, don't eat or drink anything, and don't let anyone try to pop it back in before an X-ray.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Relies on a roommate to drive to the ED and cannot mobilise to a vehicle unaided
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the roommate can drive safely and help support the arm; if no safe driver is available, arrange an ambulance (ESC-002) rather than delaying ED care.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Go to the nearest Emergency Department now for an X-ray and to have the shoulder put back in under sedation. Keep the arm supported and completely still in a sling or improvised support held against your body; do not try to move it or pop it back in. Do not eat or drink anything. Have your roommate drive you and keep the arm resting heavily in the support.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Private vehicle to the ED now with the roommate driving; call 000 if the hand becomes pale, cold, blue, pulseless or completely numb, or if safe transport is not possible.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: The Emergency Department is open 24 hours — go now, do not wait for clinic hours. If the limb changes colour or sensation, or you can't get there safely, call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The ED will provide a discharge summary after reduction; arrange orthopaedic and physiotherapy follow-up through the GP once the shoulder has been relocated.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treats the injury as a routine sprain or soft-tissue problem, advises home management or a routine appointment, and misses the need for urgent imaging + sedated reduction and the axillary nerve involvement.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Prolonged dislocation leads to avascular necrosis and permanent cartilage damage, worsening/persistent axillary nerve deficit, and a possible missed fracture-dislocation.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directs the patient to a GP or urgent-care setting that cannot perform pre-reduction imaging or procedural sedation, delaying definitive care.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calls an emergency 000 ambulance for a haemodynamically stable, well-perfused dislocation that could be safely self-transported — justified only if the limb becomes neurovascularly compromised.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot palpate the joint, formally test the nerve, or obtain an X-ray, so a concomitant fracture-dislocation cannot be excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to ED for imaging and reduction, warn explicitly against home reduction, and set a clear 000 trigger for distal neurovascular compromise.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Attempting to confirm reducibility remotely, coaching a home relocation, or reassuring the patient that it is 'just a dislocation' that can wait.
